data.title: EXG: Attractively Valued Global Income Fund (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As the Santa rally continues, U.S. market indices continue to hover near their all-time highs. Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG) provides investors with a way to reduce reliance on U.S. markets by allocating a sizeable portion of its assets to global securities outside of the U.S. When I previously covered EXG, I issued a hold rating due to the flawed dividend history. However, EXG released an updated annual report for 2025, so I wanted to take a closer look at the fund's overall value proposition for long-term investors. I believe that I may have been too strict on my expectations of the fund at the time of my first coverage and wanted to give it another honest look as we approach the new year.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that EXG's share price has increased by about 13%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 23.7% over the same time frame. The awesome total return can be attributed to the starting dividend yield of about 8.3% and monthly payouts. This makes the fund an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation from a global portfolio of equities.

While the fund prioritizes income generation, EXG relies on the growth of its equities and the included option writing strategy to support the distributions. This means that EXG will perform better during periods of positive market momentum while struggling to offer protection against severe declines. However, EXG trades at an attractive price-to-NAV valuation and may be a way for investors to reduce reliance on U.S. equities going forward. The fund has a mixed dividend history but a strong performance of out-earning its distributions. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that EXG implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, EXG has total net assets of $2.9B that are spread across 92 different positions. EXG takes a very specific approach to its portfolio and doesn't try to replicate the performance of the S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100. The fund has management fees of 0.99% of assets, which is fine considering the higher yield offered to investors. As the name implies, the fund also provides exposure to equities that have primary operations outside of the U.S. For reference, the fund's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • North America: 55.69%
  • Europe: 34.33%
  • Asia/Pacific: 7.56%
  • Latin America/Caribbean: 1.56%
  • Africa: 0.86%

Expanding on this diversity, EXG also provides instant exposure to a wide range of sectors. EXG is most heavily allocated to Information Technology sector at 24.84% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which accounts for 17.94% and 13.06% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up more than 55% of EXG's total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors will have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price change.

Additionally, the fund chooses to implement an option writing strategy against a portion of its assets in an effort to amplify the income received. Rather than including debt investments, the option writing strategy allows the fund to harness the volatility of its holdings and turn this into a positive trait. However, the fund generally keeps the option writing at a low frequency. For instance, the option writing characteristics are as follows:

  • EXG only writes options against 15% of its portfolio.
  • Written options have an average expiration of 14 days.
  • The fund estimates to only capture 0.3% of the price upside for written options.

While options can help generate income, they cap the upside growth momentum during market rallies. This can offset the fact that EXG is exposed to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, Microsoft (MSFT) is the largest holding at 5.83% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable holdings such as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and ASML Holdings (ASML), just to name a few.

Valuation & Performance Risks

Since EXG is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, EXG now trades at a discount to NAV of 6.55%. For reference, EXG has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.1% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund still trades at a reasonable price-to-NAV level in relation to its historical range. Although the discount may not be as attractive as it once was a few years ago, I think that the current levels are still a solid entry point for long-term investors.

However, I wanted to review the latest annual report for 2025 to get more clarity into the fund's internal performance. Net investment income amounted to $0.06 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.31 per share. Looking back on prior years, EXG has historically reported weak net investment income levels. This is an indication that the fund doesn't really prioritize interest or dividend income as a way to fuel its earnings growth and NAV growth. Therefore, the fund is ultimately reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge this gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. EXG relies on positive market momentum so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Conversely, market declines will likely result in large net realized losses. This means that EXG doesn't really offer much protection against market declines. This vulnerability has resulted in stunted NAV growth during periods where market indices declined. Despite this, the fund has demonstrated heavy long-term growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $10.36 per share
  • 2022: $7.81 per share
  • 2023: $8.00 per share
  • 2024: $9.20 per share
  • 2025: $9.78 per share

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to technology companies, I anticipate that EXG will reward investors that are bullish about the outlook of the sector. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can expand at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. While EXG can participate in this expansion since it has direct equity exposure to many of the companies leading the AI charge, long-term investors should limit their expectations. Due to the option writing strategy and the fact that the fund needs to sell off a portion of its gains to fuel distributions, the total returns are likely to underperform more traditional index-tracking ETFs.

For instance, the iShares Global 100 ETF (IOO) offers a straightforward way to get equity exposure on a global range. When comparing the performance over the last ten years, we can see that EXG's total return severely underperforms against IOO. Therefore, it's important that investors understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time.

As previously noted, EXG is heavily reliant on net realized gains to support distributions. This means that EXG may severely underperform in a scenario where indices fall. If the fund cannot produce earnings that outpace distributions, EXG will find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually generates. This can lead to much larger losses than traditional ETFs that do not prioritize distributions. A good example of this can be demonstrated through 2022's market decline. As we can see below, EXG saw a much larger loss than IOO, since it is still paying out a large rate of distributions.

Variable But Solid Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0657 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.7884 per share. When referring to the last annual report, we can see that the fund may see some variability in its ability to produce earnings that exceed the distributions being paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $3.03 per share
  • 2022: ($1.72) per share
  • 2023: $0.85 per share
  • 2024: $1.94 per share
  • 2025: $1.37 per share

So while the earnings can wildly vary based on market conditions, I believe that EXG is likely to continue supporting the distributions. Don't be fooled by the downward trend of payouts on the graph below. I believe that the variable payouts are a result of strong management that remains proactive in keeping the NAV secure over a long period of time. The fund has already demonstrated its ability to produce several years worth of earnings in a single period. A good example of this was demonstrated in 2024:

2024's earnings of $1.94 per share / $0.7884 annual payout = 2.46 years.

One of the most attractive aspects of the fund is the ability to pay out the majority of its distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 73.5% of the payouts YTD were fueled by net realized long-term capital gains. This means that most of the income received YTD was classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. Therefore, EXG can be a great option for investors that want to generate income outside of a tax-advantaged account.

However, tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily equate to tax-free. For instance, 20.2% of distributions YTD were supported by net realized short-term gains, which are taxed at the highest rates for investors. Similarly, a smaller portion of payouts is also supported by net investment income, which also has the least favorable tax treatment. So investors that want to absolutely minimize their tax liability should utilize a position within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on EXG to a buy. After reviewing the latest annual report, the fund has demonstrated its ability to produce positive earnings that can support distributions. The fund still trades at a very reasonable price-to-NAV valuation that indicates some upside potential if AI continues to lead technology stocks higher. However, the caveat is that a position in EXG makes the most sense for investors that specifically want income generation. If you're an investor that simply wants the highest total return from a global portfolio of equities, a more traditional growth ETF may be the more efficient choice.

data.title: DGRO's 2025 Reconstitution Highlights Strong Dividend Growth
data.description:
Recap

I last covered iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) about a year ago for its 2024 annual reconstitution, and I'm back again covering this year's 2025 reconstitution. I added heavily to my DGRO position in 2025 and will continue doing so in 2026. Year to date, DGRO has performed well, delivering 15.6% returns while mostly keeping up with the S&P 500's 17.5% with less volatility. Both smoked my Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) position, but I don't mind as I own all three.

I was pretty nervous in the second half of the year about whether DGRO would be able to deliver yet another year of uninterrupted dividend growth, and it did, delivering an 18% increase compared to last year's Q4 dividend and a 4.73% annual increase overall for the year. Not the strongest of dividend growth years, but hey, it's more than my annual W-2 raises will give me. In any case, DGRO's methodology delivered yet again a solid year of performance with its methodology and annual reconstitution when they clean house.

Reconstitution Analysis

Let's dive into DGRO's 2025 annual reconstitution. For a refresher on the exact methodology, please refer to my reconstitution article from last year. For a quick recap, DGRO focuses on companies with consistent dividend increases and screens for sustainability. All holdings are US-based and pay a qualified dividend, with the top holdings being weighted by dividend dollars paid, capped at no more than 3% per holding. The holdings have been slowly reducing, which I like, from 435 in 2023, 415 in 2024, 410 in 2025, and now 406 going into 2026.

The new top 50 positions, which represent 66% of the ETF, are dominated by large-cap, blue-chip companies with a weighted average yield and DPS (dividend per share) growth of 2.24% and 7.80%, respectively. Above are the top 10 holdings, which make up 26% of the ETF. The top 30 holdings by dividend growth represent 6.7% weight with an average yield of 1.70%, lower than the overall ETF yield, with a DPS growth of 23%, reflecting dividend growth over yield. Notable dividend growers were The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), with a blistering DPS growth of 33.33% and 20%, respectively.

44 companies were added, representing about 7% of the ETF's weight, mostly driven by the five-year continuous dividend growth screen. The average yield is in line with the majority weight at 2.59% and slightly higher dividend growth of 8.51%. Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) stood out as pretty notable adds. 43 companies were removed, representing about 5.4% of the ETF's weight. The removals were mostly driven by "extreme dividend yield." An interesting insight is the average yield of these removed holdings was 4.34%, but their dividend growth was only 5.25%. I won't be missing U.S. Bancorp (USB), Target Corporation (TGT), Comcast Corporation (CMCSA), or General Mills, Inc. (GIS). In my opinion, these are companies that are socially and operationally on a downward trend. These adds and removals are a small weight overall, keeping turnover low and reflecting a strong prior portfolio yet again.

Overall, I believe this reconstitution emphasizes the strong dividend growth profile of DGRO. Technology and Health Care dominate the top holdings for overall growth, with Utilities and Energy keeping the yield above the S&P 500. The yield is still pretty conservative, but I believe the 2026 dividend growth is set up well to keep the uninterrupted dividend growth streak alive and possibly get into double-digit growth.

Outlook

DGRO and SCHD are a substantial part of my taxable brokerage portfolio and the real core of my income strategy. I auto-buy $50 of each ETF every single day and feel very comfortable scaling these holdings into multi-million-dollar positions one day. In fact, I don't know how else I will retire comfortably if I don't do this. At around $70/share, I think there's plenty of value even at today's entry price than there was a year ago due to the price appreciation, dividend growth, and overall growth set up for next year. One thing I do find myself getting frustrated with on DGRO is the low yield. I want a 4%+ yield now, but we have to be patient and let the yield on cost do its thing with each year's annual raise. That's why I buy NEOS funds in my business brokerage account and am trying to buy one Bitcoin via NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) to help offset this frustration and increase my current income.

While I track the total returns of my holdings against the market and other competing funds, I'm very much focused on scaling my dividends in my portfolio. My goal is highly tax-efficient passive income to offset all my fixed expenses: subscriptions, cellphone, water, internet, gas, energy, car & home insurance, CapEx, property taxes, and grocery bills, and one day, all my more variable and fun living expenses to live a comfortable and secure life.

data.title: GoGold: A Good Value Even With More Conservative Metal Prices
data.description:
Overview

GoGold Resources (GLGDF)(GGD:CA) is a Canadian-listed silver mining company with three assets in Mexico. Parral is its smallest asset, which is effectively a cleanup of old tailings that is producing around 2 million silver equivalent ("AgEq") ounces per year. The company also has the two development projects, Los Ricos South and Los Ricos North, which account for most of the company's value.

There was some optimization work a couple of years ago on Parral, which reduced production for a period, but the asset has, over the last year, been producing around 500Koz AgEq per quarter, and the silver equivalent cash cost has been around $18-19/oz over the last few years. Based on the current spot price of silver or just the $41.55/oz realized silver price in the most recent quarter, the margin is very healthy for Parral. This asset is effectively funding the general & administrative expenses and development costs of the other assets.

I have covered GoGold frequently going back to 2018, when it was first added to my portfolio, and my prior articles can be found here. I am still long the stock. The stock price is up 181% this year, which is roughly in line with the precious metals mining ETFs.

This is no doubt a healthy return for GoGold, but I still think the stock price has underperformed the expectations some in 2025. That is probably true for many silver stocks, where I would expect their returns to be more explosive over the last 6 months, when the price of silver has roughly doubled, and that does especially apply to GoGold.

I think the main reason GoGold has "underperformed" is because the pending permit on Los Ricos South has yet to be granted, and the other reason is the recent C$144M bought deal that closed in November.

There is no clear timeline of the permit approval, but the company has communicated that it is close. I think GoGold has a good chance of getting it sorted in the next 6 months following the recent open pit permit approval for Silver Tiger Metals (SLVTF), after a long period of few new mining permits being granted. The Los Ricos projects have good local support, which is key for most mining projects.

GoGold has done a good job of timing its bought deals over the years, which are highlighted in the chart below. I understand the rationale for the recent bought deal, as it will allow GoGold to move forward with both Los Ricos South and Los Ricos North quickly, without extending the balance sheet too much. With that said, I don't think a half-warrant would have been needed if management had been more patient with this capital raise. GoGold had no debt and over $100M prior to the bought deal and now has around $240M in cash on its books.

Valuation

GoGold had a feasibility study released in January of this year on Los Ricos South, making those estimates reliable. For Parral, we can quite easily discount the cash flow of its operation as well. However, the latest technical report on Los Ricos North was a preliminary economic assessment released in the first half of 2023, which makes those estimates more unreliable.

Historically, I have also assumed permitting Los Ricos North as an open pit project might be difficult, which is probably no longer the case following the recent permit approvals in Mexico. With that said, I have still been somewhat conservative with my estimate for Los Ricos North.

In the chart below, I have used two metal price scenarios and a 5% discount rate for the valuation. One could certainly make a case for a higher discount rate, but similarly for more aggressive metal prices, especially for silver. The first price scenario uses a silver price of $40/oz and a gold price of $3,500/oz; the second scenario uses a silver price of $50/oz and a gold price of $4,000/oz.

Here we can see that the total value of GoGold's various assets is $1.7B and $2.3B for the two metal price scenarios. The fully diluted enterprise value is $714M for GoGold, which means the stock is trading at 31% or 42% of total asset values depending on the price scenario we use.

Conclusion

GoGold is a relatively high-quality silver development company. The balance sheet and liquidity position are extremely good; the projected operating costs of the two Los Ricos projects are in the lowest quartile of the cost curve for silver projects. This is also a management team that has built mines before, offsetting the execution risk some.

Given how few high-quality silver projects there are with near-term production capabilities, I think this is a compelling stock price for GoGold. I rate the stock a buy, and I have been adding more money to my position in GoGold recently.

The slightly rushed recent bought deal is a minor issue in my view, which will be forgotten in a few months. The main risk is without a doubt the permitting risk in Mexico, where this discount was likely warranted a year ago but probably not anymore when mining permits have started to be issued in 2025 again. With that said, the binary risk of a mining permit on Los Ricos South is something all investors should be aware of.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P Global: Intelligence Player Intelligent Here?
data.description:
In the spring I concluded that S&P Global (SPGI) had become an impressive information powerhouse. Following the IHS Markit merger, the company has become a $14 billion information powerhouse, yet demanding valuations made me a bit cautious.

Ever since, shares have risen some 15%, and while they mark decent returns given such a period, they have been lagging the market returns by a substantial degree.

Right now shares still command premium multiples amidst solid returns seen, driven by a resilient operating performance. Given this backdrop and continued question of what AI has for an impact on the business, I am still erring cautiously here.

An Information Powerhouse

S&P Global looks to power global markets, with its information provision and services accelerating progress made in such markets. The company generates about $14 billion in sales, of which about a third is generated from market intelligence solutions and about a third from rating activities (which are a bit more cyclical), as well as commodity insight, mobility, and indices products.

The business was formerly known as McGraw-Hill, which tied its operations up with IHS Market in a $44 billion deal in 2020 (although closing only took place in 2022). The business is incredibly profitable, posting operating margins in the forties.

Back To Spring

Shares of S&P Global traded at $463 in April, already commanding premium valuations. This followed the business posting a 14% increase in 2024 sales to $14.2 billion, amidst strong operating momentum, notably in the rating business. Adjusted earnings were up a quarter to $15.70 per share; that number looked quite clean.

With net debt reported just shy of $10 billion, while EBITDA came in a long way to $7 billion, leverage was no issue in the mid-1s. The company guided for 2025 sales to advance by 5-7%, with adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $17.00-$17.25 per share.

The 309 million shares granted the company a $143 billion equity valuation in April and a $153 billion enterprise valuation. This is equal to about 10 times sales and 27 times adjusted earnings going forward. The company furthermore announced the sale of its 50/50 joint venture OSTTRA, this unit finding a new home at KKR. Given a $3.1 billion deal tag, S&P Global was set to fetch $1.5 billion in proceeds, making this a bolt-on divestment amidst continued streamlining efforts post the mega merger.

Simply, the company has a sticky business model, one with a solid track record, all commanding premium valuations. Having proven naysayers wrong for a long period of time, I feared the setup of high expectations and margins, together with the potential overhang of AI emerging.

Modest Gains

Since the spring, shares of S&P Global have largely traded in a $450-$550 price range, now trading at $525 per share.

Along with the first quarter results in April, S&P Global announced its intention to spin off S&P Global Mobility, targeted to become a standalone public company. This is a smaller part of the business, generating about $1.6 billion in sales in 2024, those being growing sales. The anticipated spin-off into a potentially publicly separate business was considered to be completed in 12-18 months; this is likely to be seen taking place in the second half of 2026.

With quarterly results coming in line with expectations, the company announced its next M&A move in October. In the middle of the month, S&P announced a $1.8 billion acquisition of With Intelligence, a provider of private markets data, insights, and analytics. Its solutions provide data to investors, managers, and funds and cover over 300,000 deals. With a $130 million revenue contribution, the purchase is expensive at nearly 14 times sales.

By the end of the month, the company reported third-quarter results, with a net debt load reported at $9.7 billion, despite the company fetching proceeds from selling-off OSSTRA. By now the company sees sales up 7-8%, with adjusted earnings between $17.60 and $17.85 per share, aided by resilient performance and a weaker dollar.

Commanding a $170 billion enterprise valuation, S&P Global traded around 12 times sales and about 30 times adjusted earnings per share, all still demanding valuations.

These valuations, however, are driven by continued growth in combination with capital allocation strategies. The company claims that AI is a driver for the business. I still see this as a potential threat as well, making me still quite cautious, at least for now, even as the company powers on.

data.title: Comex: Physical Silver Demand Keeps The Market In Backwardation
data.description:
The CME Comex is the Exchange where futures are traded for gold, silver, and other commodities. The CME also allows futures buyers to turn their contracts into physical metal through delivery. You can find more detail on the CME here (e.g., vault types, major/minor months, delivery explanation, historical data, etc.).

The data below looks at contract delivery where the ownership of physical metal changes hands within CME vaults. It also shows data that details the movement of metal in and out of CME vaults. It is very possible that if there is a run on the dollar, and a flight into gold, this is the data that will show early warning signs.

Gold

The chart below shows the spread between the spot price of gold and the futures contract pricing. As you can see, it blew out earlier this year (increasing contango). This created the massive arbitrage that resulted in gold traveling from London to New York. When the spread between futures and spot gets distorted, it suggests there is dislocation in the market. The spread normalized some between August and early December, but has started to blow out again. It’s possible this could lead to increased delivery volume again, but we have not seen that yet this month.

Figure 1: Spot vs. Futures

As shown below, while gold delivery volume is elevated on a historical basis, it is below the three massive delivery months earlier this year (Feb, Apr, and Oct). If spreads continue to blow out, then Jan and Feb could show large delivery volume again.

Figure 2: Recent like-month delivery volume

Below is a chart that isolates the month of December going back 30 years. We can see that on a notional level, this December is absolutely smashing historical records. While this is heavily impacted by the surging price (notional is price x volume), it still shows that the appetite for gold in dollars is increasing dramatically.

Figure 3: Notional Deliveries

Net new contracts (contracts that open and settle for immediate delivery) was a modest driver. Not as large as other months, but also a late surge that is not usually seen. That surge came as the spread started to widen (Figure 1). It’s likely that these are related.

Figure 4: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Perhaps the most important data point is the actual metal leaving Comex vaults. When metal is “delivered,” it does not mean it’s leaving the Comex. It means that Registered ownership is moving from one party to another. When the metal leaves the vault, that is where things get interesting. As shown, this year saw a major influx as the arbitrage trade took hold, but that metal was flowing back out. In the latest month, a lot of Eligible was converted to Registered to satisfy delivery demand. The Comex is not (yet?) bringing in any new metal.

Figure 5: Inventory Data

Looking ahead to the December delivery period, we see a contract that is hovering near the middle range of all minor months.

Figure 6: Open Interest Countdown

With the massive surge in inventory, the open interest relative to physical stocks is actually lower. This shows how much metal has been added to Comex vaults this year.

Figure 7: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Bottom line, December saw elevated deliveries on a historical level, but well below the 2025 highs. The Comex vaults saw little metal arrive or leave this month; it was mainly a conversion of Eligible into Registered.

Silver

While the gold market is showing a futures price that is getting higher above the spot rate (which caused metal to move from London to New York), the silver market is experiencing the opposite problem, backwardation. The spot price is above the futures price. This is actually a bigger warning sign because it highlights the pressure being put on the physical spot market.

Market Signal: Backwardation often signals a strong immediate demand or a supply shortage in the current spot market. The market is willing to pay a premium for the asset now, as the shortage is typically expected to resolve over the long term.

Figure 8: Spot vs. Futures

While gold delivery volume has cooled some, the silver delivery volume remains elevated.

Figure 9: Recent like-month delivery volume

Switching to notional values, and focusing on the month of December, produces the chart below. Again, some of this is price appreciation, but it is also an increased appetite for deliveries. In 30 years of delivery data, the notional amount is almost 2.5x higher than any previous December on record. The price appreciation is not denting the demand for physical.

Figure 10: Notional Deliveries

Unlike gold, net new contracts were a major driver this month, reaching the second highest behind only September.

Figure 11: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Silver-eligible inventories are seeing some modest inflows this month, but this is not new metal... it is metal coming from Registered as we are about to see.

Figure 12: Inventory Data

Registered metal (metal available for delivery) is seeing major outflows. Since September, there has been a massive drawdown in Registered supplies. This is further evidence of strain in the physical market. Investors are taking delivery and moving it to Eligible this month. In prior months it was coming straight out of the vault. This will be the chart to watch as we head into 2026.

Figure 13: Inventory Data

As we approach January delivery, the silver contract is now at the highest open interest when compared to all prior minor months. The deviation from the trend is stark.

Figure 14: Open Interest Countdown

On a relative basis, open interest is still in record territory despite the elevated inventory levels.

Figure 15: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Conclusion

The gold market has certainly calmed down when compared to the activity earlier this year. That said, with spreads widening, we may be in store for some more fireworks. Earlier this year, metal flowed into Comex vaults to satisfy demand that was coming from traders looking to arbitrage.

Silver is showing opposite behavior with spot prices above futures. This is causing metal to flow out of the Comex vaults. Heading into January, demand is staying very strong. While silver prices have gotten extended to the upside, any pullback should be well supported by physical demand.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the third quarter of 2025.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund returned 3.72% for the three months ending September 30, 2025. For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com .
  • The fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index (BMB), returned 3.00% for the same period.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as the fund's longer-duration profile was additive to returns. (Duration is a measure of a bond's sensitivity to changes in interest rates)

Market overview

The third quarter began with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act clearing both chambers of Congress and being signed into law on July 4 by President Trump. Throughout the quarter, tariff policy headlines continued, yet market reactions to trade policy announcements decreased relative to the previous quarter. While inflation and growth uncertainty remained, the U.S. Federal Reserve shifted its focus to the labor market, which showed signs of weakness throughout the period. The Fed viewed the downside risks to employment as meriting some degree of easing, despite inflation that remained somewhat elevated. After leaving its benchmark overnight lending rate unchanged at its August meeting, the Fed lowered the federal funds target rate in mid-September by a quarter point, to the 4.00%–4.25% range. Returns were meaningfully positive across all sectors of the U.S. bond market for the quarter. Treasury yields ended the quarter slightly lower across the yield curve, as reflected in the decline of the 10-year Treasury yield from 4.24% to 4.16% over the quarter.

Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the quarter. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 3.00%, while Treasuries were up 1.51% over the same period. Lower-quality municipals lagged higher-quality municipals as the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index (BHY) returned 1.63% during the quarter, bringing its year-to-date return to 1.29%. A dominant theme from the first half of 2025 was the reshaping and ultimate steepening of the municipal yield curve. During the third quarter, the 1–30-year AAA slope flattened by 11 basis points (bps). (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) Longer-maturity municipal bonds materially outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts.

After a modestly negative second quarter in terms of flows, fund flows increased throughout the third quarter. Open-ended funds experienced $8.3 billion in inflows, while exchange traded funds (ETFs) saw $10.1 billion in inflows for the quarter. The short and intermediate portions of the yield curve benefited most from the positive flows.

Top sector weights (%) as of September 30, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund outperformed its benchmark during the quarter. As longer-maturity bonds outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts, the fund's longer-duration profile was additive to returns. In terms of quality, security selection in AAA and AA rated bonds contributed to returns, while the fund's overweight to BB bonds detracted. Within housing and zero-coupon bonds, security selection was additive to returns, while selection in tobacco negatively impacted returns. Also detracting from returns were overweights to continuing care retirement community (CCRC) and housing bonds.

Portfolio activity

Throughout the quarter, the fund capitalized on opportunities in both the new-issue and secondary markets. Purchases were largely focused on improving the quality, liquidity and distribution yield of the fund. The strategy took advantage of attractive opportunities when volatility presented, while also decreasing duration during strong rally environments. While municipal spreads are not overly attractive from a valuation standpoint, yields remain desirable on both a historical and tax-equivalent basis. Due to ample supply in the market and higher income opportunities being offered, the fund was able to capitalize on attractive credits in several sectors. The relatively steep slope of the municipal yield curve, especially the longer end, continues to provide higher income in a stable-rate environment, as well as total return potential.

data.title: VUG: Growth Investing Is Likely To Shine Again In 2026
data.description:
Vanguard Growth ETF (VUG) is poised to deliver solid returns in fiscal 2026 as the market trends appear favourable for high-beta securities. In addition, the earnings growth in the tech category is likely to accelerate, driven by the robust demand for AI-related products. Although a valuation factor is high, the prospects for a significant S&P 500 downtrend or bear market are low compared to the previous years. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating on VUG.

A Long Lasting Uptrend Supports Growth Investing

In my previous article, I suggested that the uptrend in the growth category is long-lasting. VUG's 20% price return in 2025 vindicated my opinion. I continue to believe that the growth category's uptrend is not a bubble but a secular growth trend. It is crucial to understand that bubbles are generally formed when stock prices soar on unrealistic expectations.

So far, the current bull run is being supported by the stronger-than-expected earnings power, better-than-anticipated economic growth, and growing investor interest in tech stocks.

I believe fiscal 2026 could be one of the best years for growth investors, given a low-risk factor compared to the previous years. In the past two years, rates were around the peak levels with a higher risk of recession. On the other hand, the market fundamentals have been strengthening over the past few quarters. This is reflected in the solid earnings growth in 2025 and greater expectations for 2026.

For example, the technology sector, a key driver of the past two years bull run, is expected to report 25% earnings growth in fiscal 2025, with expectations for 28% year over year growth in the following year.

AI is likely to be a key driver of investor confidence. The mega-caps, such as NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META) and Broadcom (AVGO), are poised to benefit from the hyper AI demand. NVIDIA, which is expected to generate 56% earnings growth in fiscal 2026 and 60% in 2027, anticipates that AI ecosystem is scaling fast, with the market entering into the virtuous cycle of AI.

Microsoft also anticipates the massive opportunity ahead in the AI space. Therefore, the tech giant has been boosting its capital spending quarter over quarter. In the latest quarter, its capital spending of $34.9 billion was 74% higher than the year-ago quarter. Wall Street expects Microsoft to deliver a high teen percentage revenue and earnings growth in 2026 and 2027.

Alphabet also boosted its capital spending plan to a range of $91 billion to $93 billion for 2025 due to its investments in the AI space, compared to initial analysts expectations of around $84 billion. The company's revenue increased 16% in the latest reported quarter, while earnings topped expectations by $0.61 per share. Here is what CEO Sundar Pichai stated about AI and growth trends:

“Our full stack approach to AI is delivering strong momentum and we’re shipping at speed, including the global rollout of AI Overviews and AI Mode in Search in record time. In addition to topping leaderboards, our first party models, like Gemini, now process 7 billion tokens per minute, via direct API use by our customers. The Gemini App now has over 650 million monthly active users. We continue to drive strong growth in new businesses. Google Cloud accelerated, ending the quarter with $155 billion in backlog.”

I highlighted the top tech giants position to establish that the uptrend is being driven by strengthening fundamentals instead of unrealistic expectations. Whereas, the S&P 500 index and growth category are also likely to experience the support from low beta sectors. The financials sector's earnings are expected to increase around 9%, while industrial growth could surpass 15%. Similarly, the health care sector is anticipated to experience a 12% earnings growth. The utilities, consumer staples, and materials are also forecasted to contribute to the S&P 500 earnings growth.

The Fed's easing rate policy can also increase investors interest in risky assets. At present, the Fed rate is around 3.50% to 3.75%, down from its recent peak of 5.25% to 5.50%. The market currently anticipates more rate cuts in 2026 to end the year in a range of 3% to 3.25%.

On the other hand, the economic growth was significantly stronger than initial expectations in fiscal 2025. In the third quarter of 2025, the US GDP surged 4.3% year over year compared to expectations of a 2.5% increase. The easing monetary conditions can increase business and consumer spending in 2026.

VUG's Targeted Exposure Can Increase Returns

There are numerous investment vehicles in the growth category to capitalize on the potential uptrend. However, Vanguard Growth ETF stands out because of its targeted exposure to a large cap growth category, low expense ratio, and robust liquidity.

VUG’s portfolio is highly concentrated in the fast-growing technology, communication, and consumer cyclical sectors, collectively making up nearly 80% of its overall weight. The healthcare, financial, and industrial stocks represent a smaller weight in its portfolio.

VUG's stock-related concentration is also high, as its top 10 holdings account for 61% of its portfolio weight. Moreover, the fund's top 3 holdings represent 31% of its portfolio weight. Although a concentrated portfolio carries a higher risk factor, it can enable investors to earn greater returns. The fund's significant concentration in mega- and large cap tech giants fuelled its 135% in total return in the past three years, beating the S&P 500 return of 80%.

Other notable factors include a low expense ratio. Its expense ratio of 0.04% is significantly below the 0.50% median of all ETFs. The fund's assets under management surpassed $350 billion, while the average share trading volume is around 1.15 million. VUG’s high liquidity suggests greater ease in buying and selling than the low liquidity ETFs.

VUG Valuation and Risk

The fund's valuation appears expensive. Its trailing price-to-earnings ratio of around 40x is higher than its 5- and 10-year averages. In addition, its stock price is also trading around a record high of $500 per share.

Here the question arises whether it is the right time to initiate a new position or boost the existing share count. In my opinion, waiting for a significant price decline may lead investors to miss the potential gains.

High valuation alone may not hinder the uptrend. In particular, when earnings growth, economic expansion, and rate cuts support the bullish movement. The geopolitical tensions, trade wars, and tail events can create a big price swing. However, waiting for those unexpected events may not be a prudent strategy.

VUG may not be the right option for the low- to modest-risk-tolerance investors. The fund carries an aggressive risk factor. Its beta stands around 1.26, and a standard deviation of 16 is significantly higher than the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

The growth category has the potential to continue its market-beating performance in 2026. The uptrend is likely to be supported by the category’s earnings growth power, low-interest rate environment, and economic expansion. Specifically, the tech sector's growth rate acceleration is one of the biggest catalysts. The communication and consumer cyclical sectors are also expected to fuel the uptrend.

Vanguard Growth ETF is a worthwhile investment option because of its concentration in the mega- and large-cap tech stocks. I believe mega- and large-cap tech stocks are well-positioned to benefit from bullish trends due to their leading positions in the AI space and ability to invest significantly in growth opportunities.

data.title: Fidelity Dividend Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the third quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 8.41%, topping the 8.12% advance of the benchmark S&P 500® index.

U.S. stocks rallied in the third quarter, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reduction since December. Amid a favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed September just below its all-time high. Growth stocks continued to lead in Q3, while smaller-cap shares outpaced large-caps, signaling a broadening of market leadership and increased investor appetite for risk.

Stocks entered July on an uptrend, with the S&P 500® index gaining 24.92% from April 9 through midyear. The rally was sparked by a 90-day pause on most tariffs announced a week earlier by the Trump administration. Momentum carried into Q3, supported by strong corporate earnings and a resilient U.S. economy – largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech companies. Renewed expectations for Fed rate cuts also supported stocks. On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points, shifting from its wait-and-see stance. The decision reflected concerns about labor-market softness, which outweighed a rise in inflation – up 2.9% year-over-year in August, the highest reading since January. As of September 30, the market expects continued monetary easing into 2026 and beyond.

Leading the index in the third quarter were the growth-oriented information technology (+13%) and communication services (+12%) sectors, reflecting the transformative potential of generative artificial intelligence. Consumer discretionary (+10%) outperformed as well. The much smaller utilities sector gained roughly 8%, boosted by AI-driven demand for electricity to power data centers. In contrast, defensive-oriented stocks trailed the broader market: consumer staples returned -2%, while health care and real estate gained 4% and 3%, respectively. Other notable laggards included financials (+3%), materials (+3%) and industrials (+5%). Lastly, energy rose 6%, despite a decline in the price of oil.

Stock selection in industrials, communication services, real estate and materials accounted for most of the fund's outperformance of the benchmark. In technology, our picks added value that was offset by underweighting the strong-performing tech hardware & equipment segment.

Out-of-index exposure to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing was a key individual contributor. The fund's stake here gained 23% the past three months, rallying amid strong demand for AI processors. The firm's Q2 business update, released in mid-July, surpassed consensus estimates across all metrics, stemming from growth in AI-related demand, as tech firms continued to spend on building data centers. The world's largest contract chipmaker raised its full-year forecast for revenue growth but also struck a cautious tone as it expressed uncertainty about unfolding tariff policies.

An overweight in Western Digital also paid off, gaining 88% the past three months. In late July, the data-storage company reported notably better-than-expected quarterly revenue and earnings, fueled largely by favorable supply-and-demand dynamics in the hard-disk-drive industry. Management noted strong demand from its cloud/hyperscale customers amid tight supply.

Conversely, positioning in the financials and energy sectors detracted for the quarter. A sizable underweight in Apple was the portfolio's biggest individual relative detractor. The stock advanced roughly 24% in the third quarter. On July 31, the consumer electronics giant reported quarterly revenue of $94 billion – up 10% and a Q2 record – as well as earnings that rose 12%, exceeding market expectations. As the quarter progressed, shares of the firm were further boosted by signs of high demand for the iPhone® 17 device, which began shipping on September 19 and quickly sold out on the company's website.

Avoiding Tesla, which gained about 40% for the three months, also hurt. The stock initially declined following the company's second consecutive quarterly revenue drop, which fell short of investor expectations amid mounting competition from makers of lower-cost electric vehicles. However, Tesla regained momentum late in the quarter, supported by CEO Elon Musk's $1 billion purchase of company stock in the open market – a move widely viewed as a strong signal of confidence in the company's long-term prospects. ■

Outlook and Positioning

We remain focused on areas of the economy where we see the strongest business fundamentals, especially the industrials and energy sectors. With that said, many questions remain about the health of the U.S. economy. Overall, economic growth has been reasonably good and unemployment remains contained, but some trends worry us, such as the stalled housing market, possible fallout from higher tariffs and a weakening labor market.

In industrials, we like the commercial aerospace area. Supply chains in the group have eased and demand for widebody jets has picked up, reflecting a global recovery in air traffic. A key holding here is Boeing, the fund's No. 1 overweight at the end of September. The company is still working to resolve some longstanding production issues. With that said, in our conversations with Boeing's airline customers, we've heard favorable comments about improved quality and on-time deliveries. Additionally, the firm raised roughly $24 billion of capital in the fall of 2024 by selling additional shares of stock, helping to strengthen its balance sheet. All told, we're anticipating better times for Boeing.

GE Vernova was another meaningful overweight in the industrials sector at quarter end. The company is a global leader in the electric power industry. Its largest division makes gas turbines, which are used to generate electricity. Amid the global push toward electrification and decarbonization – including supplying the world's increased power needs due to the advent of generative AI – Vernova appears well positioned.

The energy sector also was a significant overweight as of September 30. The rationale here is reasonable valuations, corporate policies about returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks, and the steady albeit slow growth in global energy consumption. In this sector, the fund is focused on mission-critical infrastructure providers and exploration & production firms with low decline rates and predictable capital spending policies. Individual energy positions of note at quarter end were Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Energy Transfer (ET).

Information technology was our largest sector underweight at the end of Q3. Within technology, the portfolio held a sizable underweight in technology hardware & equipment, mainly because of Apple, mentioned earlier. Although the stock did well in the third quarter, we remain cautious about the longer-term outlook for it, given ongoing tariff concerns and intense competition in the smartphone market. The fund also remained underweight the software & services segment, but overweight the semiconductors & semiconductor equipment industry.

Consumer discretionary was another significant sector underweight at quarter end. In line with our broadly cautious outlook about some parts of the economy, we favored companies in other sectors. Notable benchmark components in the consumer discretionary group not held in the fund were Amazon.com and Tesla – neither of which pays a dividend – as well as Home Depot (HD).

Overall, top individual overweights at the end of September included Boeing, GE Vernova and Meta Platforms. Top non-benchmark positions were Brookfield, Taiwan Semiconductor Company and Somnigroup International. The top-three underweights were Apple, Amazon.com and Tesla.

As always, we thank you for your confidence in us and in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities. ■

Fidelity® Dividend Growth Fund

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Dividend Growth Fund is a diversified domestic equity strategy with a large-cap core orientation. The fund seeks capital appreciation.

The fund invests in a mix of large- and mid-cap stocks that have favorable prospects to sustainably pay and grow dividends over time.

Our investment philosophy centers on comparing price and value. We believe price will converge with value over time in a competitive market. Quality is an integral part of our assessment of value.

We also believe that companies with a history of growing dividends demonstrate superior risk-adjusted returns over the course of a market cycle.

A disciplined approach combining fundamental analysis, quality, valuation and accelerating capital return can help lead to outperformance over time.

Characteristics

data.title: Harbor Transformative Technologies ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“We continue to believe that transformative technologies offer growth potential not found in other areas of the market.” - Jennison Associates, LLC

Market in Review

U.S. equity markets delivered robust gains in the third quarter of 2025, fueled by continued momentum in the development of artificial intelligence (“AI”) and resilient corporate earnings. The Nasdaq 100 Index outperformed the broader market, as investors continued to seek out innovative technology and AI exposure. We continue to believe that transformative technologies offer growth potential not found in other areas of the market.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter, the Harbor Transformative Technologies ETF (“ETF”) returned 10.15% (NAV), outperforming its benchmark, the Nasdaq 100 Total Return Index, which returned 9.01%. Performance was driven by companies focused on solutions for businesses and industries undergoing rapid digital transformation, especially in fields like AI, cloud, connectivity, and data processing.

Contributors and Detractors

Performance was aided by exposure to AppLovin, Arista Networks, Impinj, TSMC, and UCB.

AppLovin (APP) has seen strong advertising revenue growth driven by its AI-powered Axon Ads Manager and rapid expansion into e-commerce and international markets. Arista Networks (ANET) benefited from accelerating revenue growth (its highest rate in two years). Growth was broad-based across cloud, AI, enterprise, and campus. Impinj (PI) operates a cloud connectivity platform. It continues to evolve its product portfolio and expand use cases in a large market opportunity. TSMC (TSM) delivered impressive profitability and raised full-year revenue growth guidance, as AI demand remains strong. UCB (UCBJF)(UCBJY) is enjoying strong demand for Bimzelx. Sales continue to outperform investor expectations in a rapidly growing market.

Notable detractors included monday.com, Dexcom, Trade Desk, HubSpot, and Arm Holdings.

Shares of software application developer monday.com (MNDY) declined due to caution around customer growth, as less effective Google search engine optimization (caused by AI search share shift) had an impact on the company’s inbound traffic. This is part of a broader concern impacting the software sector. Dexcom lost value due to concerns about the G7 continuous glucose monitor’s accuracy and performance, as well as rising competition. Trade Desk’s (TTD) share price was pressured by advertiser pullbacks related to tariffs, slowing growth, intensifying competition, and a sudden chief financial officer transition. HubSpot (HUBS) declined on competitive concerns from OpenAI's Tailor Assist. We believe HubSpot has a differentiated strategy with a platform advantage. Arm Holdings (ARM) underperformed due to short-term smartphone and other smart device weakness, though data center trends are resilient.

Buys and Sells

We initiated a position in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), a leading provider of high-performance computing and AI solutions. The company’s sharpened product road map and momentum with major cloud providers highlight its growth prospects. Continued innovation in data center and AI accelerators, broad adoption across premium markets, and disciplined execution position the company as a compelling long-term investment in rapidly expanding technology markets.

Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) designs and manufactures low-power field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), which are adaptable to a wide range of applications and system requirements. A new management team has revamped its entire road map, bolstered its supply chain, and expanded into new markets, which could potentially allow the company to accelerate revenue and expand margins.

We initiated a position in Shopify (SHOP), a global leader in e-commerce platforms that enable merchants to seamlessly sell online, in-store, and across social channels. Shopify’s intuitive, all-in-one platform supports rapid merchant growth and international expansion. With a strong history of innovation, improving profitability, and a robust recurring revenue base, Shopify is potentially positioned to capture significant share in the expanding global e-commerce market.

Adobe (ADBE) was sold due to concerns that competitive offerings (such as Canva) are gaining traction versus its Creative Cloud suite of applications, as well as concerns that AI could cannibalize the suite.

Marvell (MRVL) was eliminated due to concerns over AI revenue growth.

We eliminated the position in Trade Desk as slowing growth, coupled with increasing competition and recent management changes, has created greater uncertainty around the company’s future.

Overweights and Underweights

The ETF’s largest sector exposure and overweight relative to the index remains Information Technology. Within the sector, semiconductor and software are the largest allocations. The ETF also has significant exposure to Communication Services but is underweight relative to the index. Portfolio allocation is driven by bottom-up security selection.

Outlook

We continue to believe that transformative technologies offer growth potential not found in other areas of the market. Our areas of focus are:

AI infrastructure: AI remains the catalyst for a new era of technological advancement. Pioneering firms such as NVIDIA (NVDA) are at the vanguard, while TSMC plays a pivotal role as the world’s largest semiconductor foundry, manufacturing the advanced chips that power AI, cloud computing, and consumer technologies. Rising energy constraints, supply chain diversification, and growing interest in edge AI are increasingly shaping deployment priorities.

Cloud computing: The cloud is no longer just a place to store files. It is the foundation for everything from streaming entertainment to running multinational businesses. Enterprises are moving more of their operations to the cloud, with AI-enabled services and cost optimization driving the next leg of adoption and efficiency gains.

Digital Transformation: Businesses are rewriting the rules of engagement, using AI, automation, and data analytics to deliver faster, more personalized experiences. From small start-ups to global giants, the push to go digital is deepening, with AI-native workflows moving from pilots to production and clearer return-on-investment expectations guiding budgets.

Consumer technology: The gadgets in our pockets and on our wrists are getting smarter, sleeker, and more intuitive. While growth is moderating in some device categories, innovation continues in AI-powered features, next-generation wearables, and immersive experiences, reinforcing the shift toward more personalized and context-aware computing.

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 35-Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

data.title: Allspring Core Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG) returned 2.03% during the third quarter of 2025. Yields across the curve were lower throughout the period after another quarter of tariff news and labor concerns, which drove the Federal Reserve (Fed) to finally cut rates.
  • 10-year U.S. Treasury yields fell by 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) to end the quarter at 4.15%, down from 4.57% where it ended 2024. The 2s/10s curve steepened 3 bps throughout the quarter, ending at 54 bps. The option-adjusted spread on the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Index (LBUSTRUU) tightened by 9 bps during the third quarter to 70 bps over U.S. Treasuries.

Market review

Growth in the U.S. economy has stabilized, with uncertainty remaining high. Following a slowdown in the first half of the year, personal consumption and private investment were at least stable exiting the third quarter. Personal incomes have demonstrated resiliency, supporting consumption, and public spending and private investment have contributed modestly to growth. Inventories and net exports have grown modestly after substantial shocks early in the year, and auto sales stayed strong ahead of the expiration of tax credits.

Measures of inflation have improved slightly, and the outlook for tariffs still seems uncertain, even as inflation expectations remain anchored. Core personal consumption expenditures inflation has only modestly improved on a monthly basis, resulting in a still-high 2.9% year-over-year rate. Services and housing inflation has been slow to improve, and goods have contributed to inflation, with some categories now showing impacts from tariffs.

Financial markets gained additional strength from the prospect of Fed cuts and enthusiasm around investments in artificial intelligence. Incoming economic data has looked more stable after an orderly growth slowdown but prospective risks seem to be rising again. Longer-term, market participants will need to contemplate the prospects of an ongoing U.S. economic slowdown, inflation risks, increasing data dependency, federal budget pressures in future years, a potentially weaker dollar, and a shift away from growth led by consumption to a more balanced picture of savings versus investment. Bond yields, particularly in the front and intermediate curve points, continue to look attractive, yet term premiums and risk spreads should remain volatile.

Portfolio Positioning - Portfolio Attribution* And Strategy

Security selection—credit

In credit, both the sector overweight and security selection contributed to performance during the quarter. Key contributors were concentrated in the foreign agency and sovereigns subsectors. In foreign agency, overweights to Saudi Aramco and Bank Gospodarstwa Krajowego were the primary drivers of performance, among others. In sovereigns, an overweight to Paraguay and Mexico contributed to performance. Additional single-name contributors included an overweight to Pacific Gas & Electric (utilities), Broadcom (technology), and NTT (communications). Key detractors were concentrated in the health care, pipelines, and utilities subsectors. In health care, an overweight to Eli Lilly (LLY) and UnitedHealth (UNH) detracted from performance. In pipelines, an overweight to Energy Transfer (ET) detracted. In utilities, an overweight to Exelon (EXC) also detracted from performance. Additional single-name detractors included an underweight to Panama (sovereigns) and an overweight to Charter Communications (communications), Citigroup (U.S. banks), and Meta (communications).

Security selection—agency mortgages

In agency mortgages, the sector overweight as well as positioning and security selection in pass-throughs have been the primary drivers of performance. Mortgage spreads tightened in materially this quarter, supported by a continued decline in volatility and robust investor demand, which benefited our overweight position. In security selection, the overweight to seasoned 30-year 3.0s has been the primary driver of performance on strong coupon and story performance. The underweight to Ginnie Mae mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and 15-year MBS also contributed modestly, as both subsectors lagged the broader tightening. As for detractors, positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations and select pass-through coupons detracted. Spreads on both fixed- and floating-rate structures lagged the broader sector tightening this quarter. The underweight to discount 30-years and overweight to production coupon 30-years detracted moderately as well.

Security selection—securitized

During the third quarter, our sector overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) contributed to performance as spreads tightened. Security selection across several ABS subsectors, including credit card ABS, auto loan ABS, device payment plan ABS, and Federal Family Education Loan Program student loans, also contributed while holdings of certain private credit student loan ABS and non-qualified mortgage residential MBS were small detractors on a risk-adjusted basis. Within commercial mortgage-backed securities, sector positioning detracted while holdings in single-asset/single-borrower and 5- and 10-year conduit positions contributed.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, such as transportation, food, and medical care. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used statistics for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index is a broad base, market capitalization-weighted bond market index representing intermediate term investment grade bonds traded in the United States. You cannot invest directly in an index. The Fund uses the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Index for performance comparison. The investments of the Sub-Fund may deviate significantly from the components of and their respective weightings in the benchmark.

Definition of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMI-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund's duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond's (or fund's) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.
